text: Energy system contributions (aerobic, glycolytic, and total metabolic demand) during an incremental exercise test are moderate-to-highly correlated with 10-km running performance in recreational runners.

Aerobic and glycolytic energy contributions during incremental testing are significantly correlated with running speed in the middle and final sections of a 10-km race, suggesting these measures can predict pacing behavior.
Respiratory compensation point, VO2max, and peak treadmill speed are all moderately correlated with 10-km running performance.

Assessing energy system contribution during incremental exercise testing provides an alternative evaluation variable for endurance runners beyond traditional markers like VO2max and lactate threshold.
Total metabolic demand measured during lab testing correlates with race performance, indicating that both aerobic capacity and glycolytic capacity jointly determine 10-km race outcomes.
